ACID-1.w: True — fat-soluble substances cross the lipid bilayer freely; large water-soluble ones (glucose) need channel/carrier proteins.

ACID-2.w: True — in diabetes, glucose stays high in the blood because cells can't take it up (no/ineffective insulin).

ACID-3.w: True — normal blood pH is 7.35–7.45, so pH 7 counts as acidotic.

ACID-4.w: Excess bicarbonate (antacids) raises pH = metabolic alkalosis.

ACID-5.w: CO2 + H2O → carbonic acid, lowering pH. High CO2 (a lung/'respiratory' cause) = respiratory acidosis.

ACID-6.w: True — the kidneys (retaining/excreting bicarbonate & H+) compensate for respiratory acid-base problems, and the lungs compensate for metabolic ones.

ACID-7.w: False — fast, deep breathing (hyperventilation) blows off CO2, so it causes respiratory ALKALOSIS, not acidosis.

ACID-8.r: Breathing patterns ↔ pH: Kussmaul (deep, rapid) breathing COMPENSATES for metabolic acidosis (blows off CO2 to counter excess H+). Hyperventilation CAUSES respiratory alkalosis (too little CO2). Hypoventilation (rapid shallow / slow breathing) CAUSES respiratory acidosis (CO2 accumulates).

ACID-9.r: Matches: Hypokalaemia = low blood potassium; Hyperkalaemia = high blood potassium. Some diuretics cause hypokalaemia AND metabolic alkalosis. Excess antacids → metabolic alkalosis. 'Too much CO2 blown off' → respiratory alkalosis. Arrhythmias are linked to both hyper- and hypokalaemia.

ACID-10.r: Ketones fill-in: ketones are alternative FUELS, made from fatty acids in the LIVER when glucose is short. Low INSULIN releases fat (lipolysis). If ketoacids push blood pH below 7.35 it is an emergency. Type 1 diabetics have the HIGHEST DKA risk. Cells switch to using KETO BODIES because GLUCOSE (or fatty acids) can't get into cells without insulin, so FACILITATED DIFFUSION of glucose fails, forcing the use of KETO BODIES.

ACID-11.w: True — ventilation clears CO2, preventing respiratory acidosis in severe lung disease (e.g. COVID pneumonia).

ACID-12.w: Narcotics/opioids suppress the breathing centre → CO2 builds up → respiratory acidosis.

ACID-13.r: Select all — drugs that can cause metabolic acidosis: ASPIRIN (salicylate), METHANOL, and METFORMIN (lactic acidosis). (Antacids cause alkalosis, not acidosis.)

BLOOD-1.w: Regulation: blood helps maintain pH (also temperature & fluid balance). Carrying O2 is a TRANSPORT function.

BLOOD-3.w: Fibrinogen (soluble plasma protein) is converted to fibrin threads during clotting.

BLOOD-4.w: The erythrocyte (RBC) transports oxygen via haemoglobin.

BLOOD-5.w: Blood cells are made in red bone marrow (haematopoiesis).

BLOOD-6.w: Plasma is the fluid that carries dissolved nutrients, wastes, gases and proteins.

BLOOD-7.w: RBCs live ~120 days and are broken down mainly in the spleen (and liver).

BLOOD-8.w: Eosinophils rise with parasitic/worm infections (e.g. tapeworm).

BLOOD-9.w: Basophils (and mast cells) drive allergic reactions by releasing histamine — hence antihistamines.

BLOOD-10.w: Haemostasis begins with vascular spasm = vasoconstriction, then platelet plug, then coagulation.

BLOOD-11.w: Mature RBCs have no mitochondria, so they don't use the oxygen they carry — it's all delivered to other cells ('unselfish').

BLOOD-12.w: O negative is the universal donor (no A, B or Rh antigens to react against).

BLOOD-13.w: AB+ is the universal recipient (can receive any blood) but can donate only to AB+ ('selfish').

BLOOD-14.w: No nucleus (or organelles) leaves more room for haemoglobin → more oxygen carried.

BLOOD-15.w: Low tissue oxygen triggers the kidney to release erythropoietin → erythropoiesis (more RBCs).

BLOOD-16.w: Albumin (a plasma protein) acts as a buffer — a 'sponge' absorbing/releasing H+ to steady blood pH (it also maintains oncotic pressure).

BLOOD-17.w: Blood pH is tightly held at 7.35–7.45.

BLOOD-19.w: An Rh+ baby of an Rh- mother risks Rh incompatibility (mother makes anti-Rh antibodies).

BLOOD-20.w: Type A and type B are incompatible — A has anti-B antibodies, so B blood would be attacked. Family relation is irrelevant.

BUF-1.w: Chemical buffers in the blood act within seconds — the fastest line of pH defence (lungs = minutes, kidneys = hours/days).

BUF-2.w: Proteins buffer because they have positively charged amino groups (bind OH-) and negatively charged carboxyl groups (bind H+).

BUF-3.r: Fill-in: CO2 + H2O → CARBONIC ACID → H+ + HCO3-. The H+ is mopped up by the protein HAEMOGLOBIN in red cells. pH must stay 7.35–7.45 because ENZYMES can't work in the wrong pH. A large H+ rise causes ACIDOSIS (can lead to cell death). The main plasma buffer is BICARBONATE; another name for hydrogen carbonate is HCO3-.

BUF-4.w: False — buffers keep pH stable within the NORMAL range (≈7.4), not in the acidic range.

BUF-5.w: There is no 'carbohydrate-lipid buffer'. Real body buffers: bicarbonate, phosphate and protein systems.

INTEG-1.w: In this course the skin/integument is taught as three layers: epidermis, dermis and hypodermis (subcutaneous).

INTEG-2.w: The dermis is dense IRREGULAR connective tissue (criss-crossing collagen for multi-directional strength).

INTEG-3.w: The hypodermis (subcutaneous adipose tissue) anchors skin yet lets it slide over deeper structures.

INTEG-4.w: The dermis is vascular, so it nourishes the skin; the epidermis is avascular and fed by diffusion from the dermis.

INTEG-5.w: Mitosis occurs in the stratum basale (the deepest layer of dividing stem cells).

INTEG-6.w: Keratinocytes make up most of the epidermis and its tough, waterproof outer surface.

INTEG-7.w: Dermal papillae pushing into the epidermis create the ridges we see as finger/toe prints.

INTEG-8.w: Melanocytes make melanin, which shields skin-cell DNA from UV damage.

INTEG-9.w: Apocrine glands (in hairy areas: armpits, groin) secrete a thicker, protein/fat-rich sweat.

INTEG-10.w: Sebaceous glands secrete oily sebum that keeps skin and hair soft and waterproof.

INTEG-11.w: Normal flora = harmless microbes that live on/in the skin.

INTEG-12.w: Defensins are the skin's natural antimicrobial peptides ('natural antibiotics').

INTEG-13.w: Skin synthesises vitamin D (from UV). It also protects, senses, and regulates temperature.

INTEG-14.w: In the cold, skin conserves heat by vasoconstriction and piloerection (hairs stand up to trap air).

INTEG-15.w: Wrinkles come from loss of collagen (and elastin) in the dermis with age.

INTEG-16.w: Pacinian corpuscles are deep-pressure receptors — triggered by a firm squeeze.

HEAT-1.w: Conduction = heat moving by direct physical contact between objects.

HEAT-2.w: A warm compress touching the skin transfers heat by conduction (direct contact).

HEAT-3.w: Convection = heat carried away by moving air or fluid across the skin.

HEAT-4.w: Air moving past an uncovered patient near an open window = convective heat loss.

HEAT-5.w: Radiation = heat transfer by electromagnetic (infrared) waves, no contact needed.

HEAT-6.w: An uncovered patient radiating heat to cooler surroundings = radiation loss.

HEAT-7.w: Sweating cools by evaporation — energy is taken up as sweat turns to vapour.

HEAT-8.w: As sweat evaporates it carries heat away from the skin, lowering body temperature.

HEAT-9.w: Infants have a large surface-area-to-weight ratio, so they lose heat quickly.

HEAT-10.w: A forced-air warming blanket (Bair Hugger) warms by convection (moving warm air).

HOMEO-1.w: Homeostasis = maintaining a stable internal environment despite external change.

HOMEO-2.r: Terminology: STIMULUS = a change in the variable being regulated; VARIABLE = the thing being regulated (e.g. temperature); RECEPTOR = detects the change; EFFECTOR = carries out the response.

HOMEO-3.w: The pancreas is an organ. (A system = cardiovascular; a cell = RBC/neuron.)

HOMEO-4.w: Negative feedback REVERSES a change (back to normal); positive feedback AMPLIFIES it.

HOMEO-5.w: Blood clotting is positive feedback — each step accelerates the next until the clot forms (also childbirth, lactation).

HOMEO-6.w: The nervous and endocrine systems are the body's two main control systems.

HOMEO-7.r: Fill-in: when blood glucose drops, the PANCREAS releases GLUCAGON. It travels via the BLOOD to the liver, causing the liver to convert stored GLYCOGEN into GLUCOSE, which is released to raise blood glucose back to normal.

HOMEO-8.r: Match to the low-glucose example: STIMULUS = falling blood glucose; RECEPTOR/CONTROL CENTRE = pancreas; EFFECTOR = liver; the ENDOCRINE SYSTEM carries the hormone (glucagon); EFFECT = blood glucose rises.

HOMEO-9.w: Parathyroid hormone (PTH) RAISES blood calcium (bone resorption, gut & kidney reabsorption).

HOMEO-10.w: Diseased kidneys can't make calcitriol (calcitrol) — the ACTIVE vitamin D they normally produce from D3 — so calcium absorption falls. (Vitamin D3 itself is made in skin.)

HOMEO-11.w: A positive Trousseau's sign (carpal spasm) indicates hypocalcaemia.

HOMEO-12.w: Bone resorption releases stored calcium to keep blood calcium normal when dietary intake is low.

HOMEO-13.w: IV calcium affects the heart — monitor heart rhythm (cardiac monitoring).

HOMEO-14.w: In pregnancy, intestinal calcium absorption increases to meet the extra demand.

HOMEO-15.w: Interstitial fluid is the fluid that sits OUTSIDE cells, between them (part of ECF).

HOMEO-16.w: Oxytocin stimulates uterine contractions (a positive-feedback loop in labour).

HOMEO-17.w: In the cold the skin vasoconstricts to conserve heat.

HOMEOT-2.w: The epidermis is stratified squamous epithelium.

HOMEOT-3.w: A virus isn't a cell — it's essentially a molecular particle (nucleic acid + protein coat), so it best fits the 'molecule' level.

HOMEOT-4.w: The dorsal body cavity = the cranial + spinal (vertebral) cavities.

HOMEOT-5.w: Course-specific — your listed lecturers. Verify against your class; best match is Anne, Helen and Emma.

HOMEOT-6.w: A tissue = a group of similar (same) cells performing a specific function.

HOMEOT-7.w: Hyper- (above) + therm (heat) = hyperthermia = above-normal body temperature.

HOMEOT-8.w: Largest → smallest: lung (organ) → smooth muscle (tissue) → erythrocyte (cell) → oxygen (molecule).

HOMEOT-9.w: Thrombo- (clot) + embol (plug) + -ism = thromboembolism (a clot moving in the circulation).

HOMEOT-10.w: Excretion = removal of liquid metabolic wastes (e.g. urine). (Egestion = faeces/undigested food.)

HOMEOT-11.w: In the anatomical position the palms face forward (anterior), thumbs pointing away from the body.

HOMEOT-12.w: The navel is inferior (below) the thorax.

HOMEOT-13.w: Blowing a fan at his face cools by convection — safe and effective. An ice bath causes shivering (which makes more heat).

HOMEOT-14.w: Conduction needs contact: a sheepskin lets the dog gain/keep warmth by direct contact (the others are radiation/convection).

HOMEOT-15.w: Asexual reproduction = one parent → genetically identical offspring.

OSMO-1.r: Diagram-based osmosis scenario — read from your slide: water moves toward the side with the higher solute concentration (across the selectively permeable dialysis membrane).

OSMO-2.r: Geoffrey (salty chips → dehydration): blood volume DECREASES and blood pressure DECREASES. Best modelled by the lab set-up '15% sucrose tubing in a 40% sucrose beaker' — water leaves the tube (like water leaving the blood into the saltier gut/tissue).

OSMO-3.w: Dialysis removes small waste solutes — urea and excess salts — while keeping blood cells and plasma proteins.

OSMO-4.r: Definitions: DIFFUSION = solutes move down their gradient (high → low). FACILITATED DIFFUSION = the same, but via a membrane protein (still passive). OSMOSIS = water moves across a selectively permeable membrane toward higher solute concentration.

OSMO-5.r: Filtration is a PASSIVE transport process — it uses hydrostatic (blood) pressure as the driving force and does NOT use energy from ATP.

OSMO-6.w: A cell that shrinks has lost water to a hypertonic solution (more solute outside).

OSMO-7.r: Diffusion & temperature: as temperature rises, the kinetic energy of particles INCREASES, so they diffuse FASTER. (So hot water dissolves instant coffee faster than cold.)

OSMO-8.w: In an isotonic solution there's no net water movement, so the RBC stays the same (integrity unchanged).

OSMO-9.w: 0.9% NaCl is isotonic saline. If it were more concentrated (hypertonic), cells would lose water and shrink.

OSMO-10.w: Facilitated diffusion: the carrier protein changes shape to move molecules DOWN their gradient without ATP.

FLU-1.w: Homeostasis = maintaining a stable internal environment despite external changes.

FLU-2.w: Hormones are chemical messengers released into the blood.

FLU-3.w: Negative feedback works to reduce/reverse the stimulus (back toward normal).

FLU-4.w: Hormones are slower to act than nerves but their effects last longer.

FLU-5.w: ADH (antidiuretic hormone) is released when dehydrated. (Note: 'vasopressin' is another name for ADH.)

FLU-6.w: Rising plasma osmolarity (concentrated blood) triggers ADH to conserve water.

FLU-7.w: Low blood calcium triggers PTH (parathyroid hormone) to raise it.

FLU-8.w: Calcitonin acts on osteoclasts, inhibiting them so less calcium is released from bone (lowers blood Ca).

FLU-9.w: Calcitonin is produced by the thyroid gland.

FLU-10.w: Thyroxine (T4) controls the body's metabolic rate.

FLU-11.w: The thyroid gland sits in the neck, just below the larynx (voice box).

FLU-12.w: Calcitonin (from the thyroid) lowers blood calcium by depositing it into bone.

FLU-13.w: High blood glucose stimulates the pancreas (beta cells) to release insulin.

FLU-14.w: When glucose is low, the liver converts stored glycogen → glucose and releases it (glycogenolysis).

FLU-15.w: In coffee, water is the solvent (it dissolves the solutes).

FLU-16.w: A suspension has visible particles that settle out — they haven't truly dissolved.

FLU-17.w: Water is the most important inorganic molecule in living matter.

FLU-18.w: Acids taste sour and react with metals; they are proton (H+) donors.

FLU-20.w: More H+ ions = greater acidity (lower pH).

FLU-21.w: H+ concentration is expressed in pH units.

FLU-22.w: At pH 7 the numbers of H+ and OH- ions are equal (neutral).

FLU-23.w: Blood at pH 7.4 is slightly alkaline (above neutral 7).

FLU-24.w: A buffer resists pH change by releasing or absorbing H+.

FLU-25.w: Rebreathing CO2 from a paper bag raises carbonic acid, lowering the pH back to normal (treats the alkalosis of hyperventilation).

FLU-26.w: When blood is acidic, the kidneys excrete H+ and retain (reabsorb) bicarbonate to buffer it.

FLU-27.w: Blood clotting is a positive-feedback mechanism (it amplifies).

FLU-28.w: Moving glucose from low → high concentration is uphill, so it needs active transport (ATP).

FLU-29.w: Insulin-assisted, protein-mediated, down-gradient, no-ATP glucose entry = facilitated diffusion.

FLU-30.w: An effector produces a change that influences (corrects) the stimulus.

FLU-31.w: A supersaturated solution in the body can precipitate as kidney stones.

FLU-32.w: The number of particles in a solution is measured in moles.

FLU-33.w: Diffusion is FASTER (not slower) at high pressure. It's slower with more solute, larger molecules, or lower temperature.

FLU-34.w: Osmosis = solvent (water) moving from low solute → high solute concentration.

FLU-35.w: Adding water to a closed cell raises the hydrostatic (physical) pressure inside.

FLU-36.w: Alcohol raises ECF osmolarity, so water leaves cells and they shrink.

FLU-37.w: Filtration (pressure-driven) occurs in the kidney glomeruli and in capillaries.

FLU-38.w: In a hypotonic solution, water rushes into the RBC — it swells and may burst (haemolysis).

FLU-39.w: Diarrhoea loses water and salts together = isotonic dehydration.

FLU-40.w: Pumping Na+ out and K+ in against their gradients = active transport (the Na+/K+ pump).

FLU-41.w: Water's key importance is as a solvent — its ability to dissolve so many substances (it also cushions, buffers heat, etc.).

FLU-42.w: A young woman has less body water than an infant (babies have the highest water %).

FLU-43.w: ECF = plasma + interstitial fluid + transcellular fluids. Cytosol is INTRAcellular, so it's the exception.

FLU-44.w: An electrolyte is a substance that dissociates into ions and can conduct electricity.

FLU-45.w: Sodium and chloride are the most abundant ECF electrolytes.

FLU-46.w: Na+ (high outside) and K+ (high inside) are kept opposite by the Na+/K+ pump (active transport).

FLU-47.w: The body gains water by drinking, from food (eating), and from metabolism — not by absorbing it from the environment.

FLU-48.w: Insensible (unnoticed) water loss occurs via breathing and evaporation from skin (sweating/urine are sensible).

FLU-49.w: ADH makes the kidney reabsorb water → less, more concentrated urine.

FLU-50.w: Rising plasma osmolarity stimulates the thirst centre (and ADH release).

FLU-51.w: Sodium ions are the body's 'water magnet' — water follows salt.

FLU-52.w: ADH is activated by dehydration (to conserve water).

FLU-53.w: Thirst is relieved the moment the mouth and throat are moistened (before water is even absorbed).

FLU-54.w: When ECF osmolarity falls (dilute blood), ADH release stops → more dilute urine.

FLU-55.w: Mental confusion is an early sign of dehydration.

FLU-56.w: Drinking lots of water fast dilutes the ECF, so water moves into cells and they swell (risk of water intoxication).

FLU-57.w: Hyponatraemia (low blood sodium) results from hypotonic (over-)hydration.

FLU-58.w: Low plasma protein lowers oncotic pull, so fluid stays in tissues = oedema.

FLU-59.w: Na+ is the most abundant positive electrolyte (cation) in the body.

FLU-60.w: Aldosterone makes the kidney reabsorb sodium; water follows, so fluid is retained (raises BP).

FLU-61.w: A salty meal draws water into the blood, raising blood volume and blood pressure.

FLU-62.w: Shallow breathing (hypoventilation) retains CO2 → respiratory acidosis.

FLU-63.w: An asthma attack traps air and retains CO2 → respiratory acidosis.

PH-1.r: pH scale fill-in: the pH scale measures the degree of ACIDITY of a solution — really the number of HYDROGEN ions present. It runs from 0 to 14, with 7 being neutral.

PH-2.r: pH values: strong acids (battery acid, hydrochloric acid in gastric juice) ≈ pH 0–2; weak acids (egg yolk) ≈ pH 5–6. Strong bases (drain cleaner) ≈ pH 13–14; weak bases (seawater) ≈ pH 7–8. Saliva ≈ 7 (neutral) — equal H+ and OH-. Cola contains phosphoric AND carbonic acid. Blood is slightly ALKALINE (7.35–7.45): acidosis below 7.35, alkalosis above 7.45.

PH-3.r: Lower pH = more acidic. So pH 1 is more acidic than 5; pH 3 more acidic than 9. Most alkaline = sodium hydroxide; most acidic = hydrochloric acid; neutral = saliva. Milk can't neutralise stomach acid well because it is not ALKALINE; use milk of magnesia (or baking soda) instead. Detergents are ALKALINE. An upset stomach is MORE acidic than a healthy one.

PH-4.r: Universal-indicator colours ↔ pH (read from your chart): red/orange = strong acid (~1–3), yellow = weak acid (~5–6), green = neutral (~7), blue = weak base (~8–10), purple = strong base (~11–14).

PH-5.r: Enzyme pH: salivary amylase works best in a NEUTRAL mouth; pepsinogen → pepsin is activated by ACIDIC stomach conditions; pancreatic amylase works best in a SLIGHTLY ALKALINE duodenum; trypsin works in a SLIGHTLY ALKALINE environment created by BICARBONATE ions from the ACINI cells of the pancreas.

URIN-1.r: Nephron labels (verify on your diagram): glomerulus (capillary tuft) and Bowman's/glomerular capsule (filtration), proximal convoluted tubule (reabsorption), loop of Henle (concentrates urine), distal convoluted tubule, and collecting duct (ADH-controlled water reabsorption).

URIN-2.r: Kidney labels (verify on your diagram): cortex (outer), medulla (renal pyramids), renal pelvis, ureter, and the renal artery & vein at the hilum.

URIN-3.r: Key urinary terms: FILTRATION = plasma pushed from glomerulus into the capsule; REABSORPTION = useful substances returned to blood; SECRETION = wastes added to the tubule; MICTURITION = urination; ADH = hormone that increases water reabsorption.
